incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — data extortion (TeamPCP credentials leveraged by ShinyHunters)
incidents.vector: AWS API keys harvested via the compromised Trivy scanner (CVE-2026-33634) on March 19 were reused to access the Commission's AWS account; data was later published by ShinyHunters.
incidents.impact: CERT-EU confirmed 340 GB exfiltrated from 42 internal Commission departments and 29 other EU entities, including ~52,000 outbound email-related files; stolen archive was leaked on a dark-web site.

incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — ransomware (TeamPCP / CipherForce, jointly with Vect)
incidents.vector: Downstream compromise of the Trivy supply-chain attack (CVE-2026-33634) gave attackers entry into Sportradar's environment and production credentials.
incidents.impact: Personal data on roughly 26,000 users and 23,169 athlete records exposed, plus a client table naming 161 organizations (ESPN, Nike, NBA Asia, IMG Arena) and 328 API key/secret pairs and 8 production RDS passwords; Sportradar is listed on the CipherForce leak site.

incidents.victim: Axios npm package (≈100 million weekly downloads)
incidents.vector: Attacker hijacked the lead maintainer's npm account via social engineering and pushed poisoned axios v1.14.1 / v0.30.4 packages that pulled in a plain-crypto-js dependency, dropping a cross-platform RAT on install.
incidents.impact: Malicious versions were live for ~3 hours, potentially ~600,000 downloads; scraped credentials, cloud keys and GitHub tokens; Google/Mandiant attribute to DPRK group UNC1069; widespread downstream compromise expected.

incidents.victim: Trivy / Aqua Security supply chain (1,000+ downstream SaaS environments)
incidents.industry: Cybersecurity tooling / cross-sector software supply chain
incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — data extortion (TeamPCP, collaborating with LAPSUS$, Vect, ShinyHunters)
incidents.vector: Attackers exploited a misconfiguration in Trivy's GitHub Actions to steal a privileged token in February, then published malicious Trivy releases (CVE-2026-33634) on March 19 that harvested cloud keys, SSH keys, Kubernetes tokens and cryptocurrency wallets from users.
incidents.impact: Mandiant confirms 1,000+ impacted SaaS environments with estimated 500,000 machines and hundreds of thousands of stolen secrets; cascading compromises include Checkmarx KICS, LiteLLM, Telnyx PyPI, ownCloud build systems, Mercor AI, European Commission and Sportradar.

incidents.victim: Iran (systems with Farsi locale / Tehran timezone)
incidents.attacker: Criminal gang — data extortion (TeamPCP, posing as hacktivists)
incidents.vector: Using their foothold from the Trivy supply-chain compromise, TeamPCP published a wiper payload ("CanisterWorm") delivered via an Internet Computer Protocol canister that detects Iran timezone/Farsi locale and destroys data on the host or on every node of any Kubernetes cluster it can reach.
incidents.impact: Wiper was active for a short window over the weekend; researchers could not confirm actual data destruction, but worm infrastructure has previously compromised cloud environments on Azure and AWS.

incidents.vector: Initial access suspected to trace back to credentials harvested in the Trivy/TeamPCP supply-chain compromise; LAPSUS$ attempted to sell the data via Session before releasing it for free.
incidents.impact: A ~3 GB archive was released publicly after a failed sale; Cybernews partially verified GitHub user data for internal developers, employee data across clinical research subsidiaries, and internal source-code tree structures; AstraZeneca has not publicly confirmed.

incidents.vector: Unauthorized access detected in Hasbro's IT environment on March 28; vector not yet disclosed.
incidents.impact: Hasbro filed an 8-K with the SEC warning of operational disruption; some systems were taken offline and order shipping was running on interim procedures with delays expected for several weeks.

incidents.victim: Minot, North Dakota water treatment plant
incidents.vector: Ransomware deployed against a server supporting the city's water system; group identity withheld — ransom note handed to the FBI.
incidents.impact: Affected server was unplugged and operators ran the plant manually for ~16 hours; water quality and pressure were maintained and no ransom demand was received. Incident is a reminder of ongoing targeting of US water utilities.

incidents.vector: Everest compromised a file-transfer system operated by a third-party provider that services Nissan and Infiniti dealerships in North America.
incidents.impact: Everest claims 910 GB stolen, including customer, dealership and auto-loan data; Nissan says its own systems were not compromised and is working with the vendor; Everest threatened to release data after failed extortion.

incidents.attacker: Unknown — mix of criminal and nation-state expected
incidents.vector: Active exploitation of CVE-2026-3055, a critical (CVSS 9.3) information-disclosure bug in NetScaler that leaks sensitive memory similar to earlier Citrix Bleed flaws.
incidents.impact: CISA issued an emergency directive requiring federal agencies to patch by April 2; watchTowr reported active exploitation; previous Citrix Bleed flaws were used to target government agencies and critical infrastructure.

incidents.victim: Southeast Asian government entities (TrueConf on-prem users)
incidents.vector: Attackers exploited CVE-2026-3502 in TrueConf video conferencing (the updater validation mechanism), replacing the on-prem update package on a government-run TrueConf server with a weaponized version distributed to dozens of connected entities; Havoc C2 and ShadowPad were deployed.
incidents.impact: Check Point dubs the campaign "TrueChaos"; CISA added the CVE to its KEV catalog and gave federal agencies until April 16 to patch. Dozens of government entities across one country were targeted through the trusted update channel.

incidents.victim: Israeli and UAE municipalities (and satellite, aviation, energy, maritime firms)
incidents.vector: Large-scale password-spray attacks against Microsoft 365 tenants of municipalities and private-sector entities tied to the Iran war, with three waves observed in March.
incidents.impact: Targeting correlated with cities hit by Iranian missile strikes, indicating an intelligence-gathering / battle-damage-assessment role tied to kinetic operations; activity is ongoing.

incidents.vector: Twenty distinct groups launched data-wiping intrusions, echoing prior Iranian wiper playbooks (MBR / Agrius-style activity).
incidents.impact: Israel's National Cyber Authority confirmed at least 50 organizations affected in the weeks following Israel's military campaign in Iran and warned that wiper attacks are likely to continue even after a ceasefire.

incidents.vector: Iranian-linked intrusion; specific vector not publicly disclosed.
incidents.impact: Medtech giant Stryker reported it is back up after an Iranian cyberattack disrupted operations; limited public detail on scope.

incidents.vector: Attackers used a malicious GitHub Action to steal credentials from Cisco's development environment, then accessed internal systems and some Cisco AWS servers.
incidents.impact: Attackers claim to have stolen source code and AWS credentials and are attempting to extort Cisco; investigation is ongoing.

incidents.vector: Attackers breached the Luxembourg government's mobile device management (MDM) platform and pushed malware to managed devices.
incidents.impact: More than 4,850 phones and tablets belonging to public-sector workers were infected; devices have since been updated and secured.

incidents.vector: Phishing attack against a police employee who gave attackers initial access before detection.
incidents.impact: The police say they cut off attacker access on detection and opened an investigation into what data may have been accessed.

incidents.victim: Signal users — government officials, military, journalists
incidents.vector: Operators posed as Signal support staff and tricked victims into sharing device-linking codes, then linked attacker-controlled devices to victim accounts to intercept communications.
incidents.impact: Thousands of Signal and other secure messaging accounts compromised; FBI, French and Dutch authorities issued alerts warning of the ongoing campaign.

incidents.vector: Attackers pre-positioned multisig transaction approvals obtained from Drift's security council over weeks before executing the theft.
incidents.impact: ~$280 million in crypto assets stolen from Drift; company says the attack was prepared weeks in advance.

incidents.vector: Security breach traced back to December 2025; detailed vector not publicly disclosed.
incidents.impact: Nearly 2.7 million US individuals had personal data stolen, including Social Security numbers and health plan information; state AG notifications filed.

incidents.victim: Massachusetts towns — Ashby, Dunstable, Pepperell, Townsend
incidents.vector: Cyberattack against the Patriot Regional Emergency Communications Center, a shared emergency-comms center serving the four towns.
incidents.impact: Police and fire department services and phone lines disrupted across four Massachusetts towns.

incidents.victim: Next.js applications exposed to React2Shell (766 hosts compromised)
incidents.vector: Automated scanning exploited CVE-2025-55182 ("React2Shell") — a pre-auth RCE in React Server Components / Next.js — then ran a multi-phase shell-script harvester, exfiltrating to a "NEXUS Listener" C2.
incidents.impact: Cisco Talos identified 766 compromised hosts with ~10,120 exfiltrated files including database creds (91.5%), SSH private keys (78.2%), AWS creds (25.6%), live Stripe live keys (11.4%) and GitHub tokens (8.6%).

incidents.vector: Ransomware intrusion into the port authority's IT systems; no group has claimed responsibility.
incidents.impact: IT systems disabled, port running manually; cargo loading and unloading not disrupted but back-office operations are degraded.

incidents.vector: Ransomware intrusion by a newly launched group tracked as XP95.
incidents.impact: Attackers are demanding $100,000 not to leak the agency's data; Stats SA is being actively extorted.
